data.title: The effectiveness of the Galaxy: Polymarket's fee structure remains to be seen.
data.description:
Author: Will Owens, Research Analyst at Galaxy Digital; Source: Galaxy; Compiled by: Shaw Jinse Finance

On March 30th, Polymarket officially launched its fee-based system. For several months prior, the platform only charged fees for cryptocurrency and sports prediction markets. Now, taker fees will cover almost all categories on this world's largest prediction market platform: politics, finance, economics, culture, weather, technology, etc. Only geopolitical and international events markets remain free.

The timing and intent of this adjustment are quite clear. Polymarket's recent 30-day trading volume has reached approximately $9.55 billion.

Despite frequent criticism of prediction markets, Polymarket is no longer a rudimentary experimental product. It is now a fully-fledged business backed by investment from the Intercontinental Exchange Group (ICE, the parent company of the New York Stock Exchange). The free era built its user base by incentivizing traders to inject liquidity into the market, while the paid era requires justifying its fees. Previously, Polymarket only charged taker fees for cryptocurrency and sports betting markets: a maximum effective fee of 1.56% (50% probability tier) for cryptocurrencies and 0.44% for sports betting. Starting March 30th, these rates will be increased, and the fee mechanism for newly added categories will take effect simultaneously.

Polymarket's upcoming fee structure.

The fees are not fixed, but dynamically fluctuate. The calculation formula is: the fee rate peaks when the contract price is around $0.50 (highest uncertainty), and approaches zero at extreme price levels (lowest uncertainty).

The fees are not fixed, but dynamically fluctuate. The calculation formula is: the fee rate peaks when the contract price is around $0.50 (highest uncertainty), and approaches zero at extreme price levels (lowest uncertainty).

For example, in the cryptocurrency market, a trade of 100 contracts at a price of $0.50 (50/50 chance) incurs a fee of $0.90; the same trade at $0.95 incurs a fee of $0.32. For trades with a nearly certain outcome, participants can essentially trade for free. This fee applies only to takers. Placing orders incurs no fees and participants receive daily USDC rebates from taker fee revenue. In other words, market makers not only avoid this new fee but also receive a subsidy. This fee applies only to new markets launched on or after March 30, 2025; existing markets are unaffected. As before, Polymarket does not charge any fees for deposits or withdrawals. Our Viewpoint The fee structure categorized by market type is not arbitrary. Cryptocurrency markets have the highest fees because they are the most frequently traded and highest-risk segment on the platform, with a large number of bots operating in these short-term price markets. Setting fees effectively curbs fraudulent and high-frequency trading. Sports and political markets have lower fees because they are Polymarket's core categories and key drivers of brand awareness and mainstream adoption. Financial markets offer the most generous market-making rebates, clearly designed to attract professional liquidity providers in areas with the highest demand for market depth. Geopolitical contracts are permanently free. This is the platform's core value and the area where the prediction market can produce the purest and most valuable information signals. Charging fees for geopolitical event outcomes could distort the purity of information and undermine its core value as a public good recognized by users. In terms of revenue, Pine Analytics estimates based on Dune on-chain data that, based on current order volume, after full implementation of fees, the platform's total daily fee revenue will be approximately $1.2 million, with an overall effective fee rate of approximately 0.76%. After deducting market-making rebates and referral commissions, the protocol's daily net revenue will be between $570,000 and $950,000. On an annual basis, the annual revenue range is approximately $209 million to $342 million. For comparison, Pump.fun (the popular Solana memecoin issuance platform) has a daily net revenue of approximately $1 million, and Hyperliquid has approximately $2 million. Even at the lower end of the range, Polymarket has already entered the ranks of the highest-grossing applications in the crypto industry. The optimistic scenario is clear: Fees, through market-making rebates, support deeper liquidity, and narrower spreads attract more trading volume, creating a virtuous cycle. Polymarket will grow into a truly commercially valuable platform without compromising its geopolitical informational function, which gives it social impact. The pessimistic scenario is also straightforward: Takers will now have to pay 0.5% to 1.8% for speculative bets, and retail traders and arbitrage bots who rely on a zero-fee environment may reduce their takers' volume or trade only with pending orders. However, a gradual rollout helps. Only new markets will be affected; existing positions will remain unaffected. The real test will come when old markets expire and new fee-charging markets gradually replace them. First, focus on cryptocurrency trading volume—this category has the highest fees and is the most sensitive leading indicator. If order activity remains stable here, other categories are unlikely to experience problems. Polymarket and Kalshi are currently engaged in fierce competition in the prediction market field. The effectiveness of this pricing decision remains to be seen.

data.title: Warning signs for the US Treasury market: War, geopolitical uncertainty, and financial system fragility.
data.description:
I. Current Dynamics of the Treasury Market: Rapidly Rising Yields and Increased Volatility

In late March 2026, the US Treasury market showed significant signs of pressure. The 10-year Treasury yield rose to 4.44%, an increase of about 0.40 percentage points from the beginning of the month; the 2-year Treasury yield hovered around 3.92%, with the cumulative increase this month approaching historical highs. This trend is similar to the worst performance since September 2022, reflecting a fundamental adjustment in investors' pricing of US government debt. The rise in yields is not an isolated event, but is accompanied by a simultaneous surge in volatility. The ICE BofAML MOVE index (which measures implied volatility of Treasury options) has climbed to around 112, far above historical normal levels, indicating a sharp increase in market uncertainty regarding the interest rate path.

This dual pressure (rising yields and increased volatility) has historically often foreshadowed broader market tensions. Unlike previous volatility triggered by banking crises or real estate bubbles, the core driver this time is the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East. The conflict directly pushes up energy costs, thereby reshaping inflation expectations and weakening the attractiveness of bonds as "safe-haven assets." Investors are demanding higher premiums to compensate for the added risk, which is a direct reflection of the current steepening of the yield curve. II. Weak Treasury Bond Auction Performance: Insufficient Demand Highlights Lack of Confidence Recent treasury bond auction results further confirm market concerns. The demand for 2-year Treasury bonds in late March was unusually weak, with bid-to-cover ratios lower than expected and winning yields higher than pre-auction trading levels, reaching their highest level in months. This contrasts sharply with the strong demand seen in auctions a few months earlier. Weak demand put downward pressure on bond prices, passively pushing yields higher. Analysis shows that such weak auctions often stem from simultaneous selling by foreign investors and domestic institutions, partly due to expectations of a widening fiscal deficit coupled with geopolitical risks. From a broader perspective, the approximately $30 trillion U.S. Treasury market is a cornerstone of the global financial system. The poor auction results not only reflect short-term sentiment but also highlight long-term concerns about fiscal sustainability. The Trump administration's policy vacillations (such as tariff adjustments and military actions) have exacerbated uncertainty and eroded global trust in U.S. financial governance. Investors no longer view Treasury bonds as an absolute safe haven but have begun to assess sovereign credit risk premiums. III. Significant Deterioration in Liquidity: Declining Market Depth Leads to Pricing Difficulties Liquidity issues have become the most pressing concern in the current government bond market. Market depth (i.e., the volume of orders available for trading without significant price movements) has decreased by 40%-50% compared to recent averages, with even greater declines in derivatives markets such as government bond futures. Some large Wall Street banks have temporarily shut down their automated trading systems, relying instead on manual pricing, which is extremely rare in the world's most important financial markets. Liquidity depletion and volatility reinforce each other, creating a vicious cycle. In a high-volatility environment, traders reduce their willingness to make market-making decisions, further amplifying price swings. Historical data shows that similar liquidity contractions occurred in the early stages of the COVID-19 pandemic in March 2020 or during the banking crisis in 2023, but this time the trigger is geopolitical rather than domestic events. Deteriorating liquidity not only raises transaction costs but also weakens the pricing function of government bonds as benchmark assets, which in turn spreads to the mortgage, corporate bond, and foreign exchange markets. The root cause of this pressure on the government bond market points directly to the US-Israel military action against Iran that is expected to erupt in early 2026. The conflict has led to a sharp increase in the risk of oil supply disruptions, with Brent crude oil prices rising by more than 53% since the end of February and WTI crude oil by 45%, reaching around $112 and $99 per barrel respectively. Soaring energy prices have directly pushed up inflation expectations, with the OECD warning that overall inflation in the US could reach 4.2% by 2026. As a core driver of inflation, persistently high oil prices will force the Federal Reserve to reassess its policy path. Previous market expectations of multiple interest rate cuts have largely collapsed, replaced by pricing in "higher and longer" interest rates. This shift marks a rapid transition from a post-pandemic easing cycle to a tightening environment. Geopolitical conflicts not only affect energy but also amplify global uncertainty through supply chain disruptions, further undermining investor confidence in US fiscal and monetary policies. V. The Federal Reserve's Policy Dilemma: Major Adjustments to the Interest Rate Path Under renewed inflationary pressure, the Federal Reserve faces a difficult situation. The March 2026 FOMC meeting maintained the target range for the federal funds rate at 3.50%-3.75%, with the dot plot indicating only one 25-basis-point rate cut expected throughout the year, a significant downward revision from previous estimates. Policymakers emphasized the need to observe the lasting impact of geopolitical risks on inflation and avoid premature easing that could trigger a double-dip inflation. This policy shift contrasts sharply with market expectations at the beginning of the year. The Fed may be forced to make a difficult balancing act between slowing economic growth and inflation risks. Higher interest rates will increase borrowing costs for businesses and consumers, suppressing investment and consumption. Analysts point out that if oil prices remain high, the Fed may even consider further tightening, which would put additional pressure on the already fragile Treasury market. VI. Policy Uncertainty Amplifies Market Anxiety The Trump administration's series of unilateral decisions—from fiscal expansion to military action—significantly increased the lack of policy predictability. Repeated policy reversals eroded global investors' trust in the US financial system, leading to foreign capital outflows from the Treasury market. Expectations of expanding fiscal deficits, coupled with war spending, further pushed up long-term yields. Historical experience shows that during periods of high policy uncertainty, Treasury volatility often rises in tandem, amplifying systemic risk. This uncertainty is not limited to domestic issues but also affects international relations and capital flows. Foreign central banks and sovereign wealth funds accelerated their reduction of US Treasury holdings, forcing domestic institutions to fill the gap, but insufficient liquidity limited this process. VII. Chain Reaction on the Real Economy and Financial System Rising government bond yields directly translate into higher borrowing costs. Mortgage rates and corporate financing costs rise simultaneously, suppressing real estate and corporate investment. Economic growth expectations are revised downwards, the job market faces pressure, and the unemployment rate may gradually rise. Meanwhile, the stock market becomes more sensitive to changes in bond yields, and volatility will significantly increase. A deeper impact lies in the transmission within the financial system. Cracks have already appeared in the private lending market: default rates are rising, investor redemption pressure is increasing, and banks are tightening lending in this area. As an important component of shadow banking in recent years, the pressure on private lending may first trigger localized breakdowns, and then spread to a wider range of asset classes through leverage channels. VIII. Systemic Risk Assessment and Future Outlook The US Treasury market is issuing a clear warning: the stability of underlying assets is being eroded. When the world's safest assets face the "triple pressure" of rising yields, increased volatility, and declining liquidity, the fragility of the entire financial system is laid bare. This situation differs from previous cyclical adjustments; it is the result of the combined effects of geopolitical shocks and domestic policy uncertainties. Looking ahead, if the Middle East conflict continues or escalates, oil price and inflationary pressures will further solidify tightening expectations, potentially intensifying pressure on the Treasury market. Conversely, if diplomatic efforts lead to de-escalation, the market may experience a period of recovery. Regardless of short-term developments, investors need to reassess their risk framework: diversify allocations, pay attention to liquidity indicators, and be wary of policy surprises. Overall, current signals from the government bond market remind all parties that financial stability depends on a predictable policy environment and geopolitical peace. Ignoring this foundation, no asset class can remain unaffected. Policymakers and market participants must work together to address this challenge to prevent localized pressures from escalating into a systemic crisis.

data.title: Bitcoin vs. Global Markets: Correlation Reveals True Investor Behavior
data.description:
Author: Anthony J. Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

In its early days, Bitcoin was easily seen as a side branch in a remote corner of the financial world, largely ignored. Its price fluctuations were completely uncontrollable, utterly disregarding the movements of the S&P 500, US Treasury bonds, crude oil, or gold. This low correlation was a core attraction for early participants.

From 2015 to 2019, Bitcoin's correlation with major asset classes consistently hovered near zero. The correlation coefficient with the S&P 500 is only 0.03 to 0.05, almost negligible; its correlation with bonds, crude oil, and gold is similarly weak. This is Bitcoin's purest "digital gold" phase. As a highly volatile niche asset, its holders are mostly retail speculators and idealists, largely unaffected by broad market cycles. When the stock market surges or crashes, Bitcoin often remains unmoved, living in its own independent world. Frankly, this state was extremely valuable—Bitcoin is a highly independent asset among financial assets. This independence is not accidental; from a portfolio theory perspective, it gives Bitcoin genuine practical value. An asset whose movements are unrelated to all other assets can effectively reduce overall portfolio risk regardless of price fluctuations, thus possessing significant allocation value. During that period, the logic of allocating Bitcoin based on its low correlation was very convincing. The COVID-19 pandemic became a turning point, breaking this low-correlation "curse." When the market crashed in March 2020, Bitcoin was caught in the same wave of liquidity selling as stocks, commodities, and credit assets. Investors desperately needed cash, so they sold off all assets that could be quickly converted into cash. The correlation between Bitcoin and the S&P 500 surged almost overnight from near zero to around 0.47. What followed was even more significant. As central banks injected massive amounts of liquidity into the market and the market rebounded, Bitcoin surged dramatically. But this dramatic rise was perfectly synchronized with the Nasdaq index, dominated by technology stocks, and risk-averse stocks. The correlation between Bitcoin and the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) continued to climb, eventually reaching 0.55–0.65 between 2021 and 2022. Bitcoin was thus completely integrated into the macro trading system. It ceased to be an independent asset class and evolved into a high-beta risk sentiment indicator. The investor base holding Bitcoin had changed dramatically, leading to a complete shift in its trading logic. The approval of a Bitcoin spot ETF in January 2024 marked a watershed moment. Bitcoin supporters and Wall Street institutions rejoiced at this once unimaginable milestone. However, those bullish on Bitcoin should realize that their expectations might not have been met with the expected results. A massive influx of institutional funds brought with it traditional Wall Street trading thinking, meaning the differences in trading behavior between different assets began to disappear. Investment managers relying on models for asset allocation and mechanical rebalancing began holding Bitcoin. This also meant that when they sold stocks, they often sold Bitcoin simultaneously. The voice of idealistic holders largely gave way to profit-driven hedge funds. Currently, the correlation coefficient between Bitcoin and the S&P 500 ETF is approximately 0.49, similar to levels seen at the end of 2025. During periods of high market volatility, the short-term rolling correlation has surged to 0.88. Regardless of what Bitcoin believers claim, this crypto asset is currently behaving entirely like a risk asset. This is crucial for investors hoping for a "stock market down, Bitcoin up" scenario. The reality is quite the opposite; the correlation between the two will only strengthen. Conversely, the correlation between bonds and crude oil and Bitcoin is negligible. The story of bonds is simple because there is virtually no correlation. Take the US Long-Term Treasury ETF (TLT) as an example: The correlation between Bitcoin and long-term US Treasury bonds has historically been close to zero, currently around -0.01. Bitcoin is a risk-seeking asset, and bonds are a safe-haven asset, but their relationship is extremely volatile and weakly correlated, lacking strategic allocation value. The price movement of crude oil is similar. A 10-year study by Binance Research shows that there is no significant long-term correlation between Bitcoin and crude oil prices. Although there may be short-term synchronized surges, they quickly fall back, with a long-term correlation coefficient of only around 0.15, so weak that it can almost be considered uncorrelated. The relationship between Bitcoin and gold, however, saw a truly anomalous change in 2026. Historically, for most of the time, Bitcoin and gold have had a weak positive correlation, typically between 0.10 and 0.35. The market logic is that both are sensitive to a weakening dollar and inflation concerns, a narrative that has supported the marketing slogan of "digital gold" for many years. But looking at this chart reveals: The correlation between Bitcoin and gold has completely collapsed. Last year, gold surged due to geopolitical safe-haven demand, while Bitcoin experienced a sell-off. This means the two assets almost moved in opposite directions. This is one of the very few times in history that two major stores of value have shown a negative correlation. This trend continues this year. Currently, the correlation coefficient between Bitcoin and gold is -0.69, marking the most severe divergence on record. The only change is that since the bombing of Iran on February 28th, gold has begun to fall, while Bitcoin prices have continued to rise. Even though the two assets have reversed their directions, the negative correlation remains strong. So what conclusions can we draw from this data? First, Bitcoin has matured and is being accepted by larger pools of funds. These new investors, regardless of their asset class, have directly incorporated Bitcoin into their familiar risk-averse/risk-preferential trading frameworks. Today, Bitcoin is simply following the macroeconomic trend. Bitcoin's integration with the financial system is becoming increasingly close, and its response to the macroeconomic environment is becoming more sensitive. Unfortunately, its value as a portfolio diversification tool has declined significantly. Whether this is evolution or regression depends entirely on your original perception of Bitcoin. There will always be staunch Bitcoin believers in the market who will never sell, regardless of market conditions. However, I am increasingly convinced that many early Bitcoin holders chose to sell last year precisely because they realized that the once unconventional, rebellious asset worth betting everything on was gone. What remains is merely a slightly more volatile cryptocurrency, hoping to attract more capital in the coming years.

data.title: The focus of AI payment is not the payment itself.
data.description: "Reorder the milk tea I bought from that shop yesterday." If one day you could really say this to your phone, and it would find the merchant, confirm the product, place the order, pay, and arrange delivery, you probably wouldn't first think of a payment revolution. You'd mostly just think that your phone is finally starting to act as a capable assistant. This might be a more appropriate starting point for our discussion of AI payments today. Most people's understanding of AI payments isn't about giving AI a wallet, nor is it about letting a robot decide how to spend money. What people truly experience is whether a single sentence can directly become a transaction. You state your needs, and the system handles the rest. Therefore, what's truly worth discussing about AI payment isn't the word "payment" itself, as that term is too abstract and distant. For most internet users, the immediate experience might be: an app or AI assistant can handle your shopping needs directly when you express them in plain language. If we only look at the Chinese market, AI payment isn't actually that unfamiliar. The reason is simple. Chinese users already live within a very mature mobile internet ecosystem: food delivery, e-commerce, maps, ride-hailing, local services, mobile payment, and instant delivery—these things are inherently interconnected. Often, what users want isn't a completely new payment method, but rather to avoid having to switch between apps and repeatedly click the same buttons. In China, AI payment is more like an upgrade to the e-commerce shopping experience than a complete rebuild of the payment system from scratch. Alibaba's upgrade of the Qwen App in January this year exemplifies this direction. According to the official introduction, Qwen has integrated with services within the Alibaba ecosystem, including Taobao, Taobao Flash Sale, Alipay, Fliggy, and Gaode Maps. Users can order food, complete payments in chat, and plan and book trips with just a sentence. It seems like AI has become smarter, but more accurately, it's the existing capabilities of the platform that have been reorganized. This is why AI payment is most easily implemented in China not through grand visions, but through everyday scenarios. For example, "Please buy another box of the printer paper I bought last week and deliver it to my office." Or, "Order another delivery from that restaurant yesterday and have it delivered to my home." Or, "Book a restaurant close to the office, for four people, with a budget of under 500 yuan." Users understand these prompts immediately. Because the AI ​​here isn't some mysterious new financial tool; it's simply a smarter shopping assistant. Whatever the people need, the endlessly competitive Chinese internet giants will do it. Today, there are roughly three implementation paths surrounding AI payments. One is to let AI watch the screen and operate on your behalf, clicking buttons, filling in information, and submitting processes like a human. The advantage of this approach is that it can be tested anywhere, but the disadvantages are also obvious: problems easily arise when the page changes, and key parts often require your final confirmation. For example, the Doubao phone, which was immediately targeted by WeChat upon release. Another approach is for the platform to simply hand over its service capabilities directly to AI. For large Chinese companies, this path is actually the smoothest. Because payment, merchants, maps, orders, and delivery are already under their control. AI doesn't need to mimic user clicks; it can directly access the capabilities within the system. In terms of user experience, it's "I said something, and it actually did it for me." Examples include Alibaba's Qianwen and ByteDance's Doubao. The third approach is even more difficult, namely the cross-platform, cross-website, and cross-company approach. At this stage, the issue is not just whether the product is easy to use, but whether others are willing to let your AI represent the user to perform this task. Currently, no Chinese internet company seems to be attempting to challenge this "hard" model. Today, China's greatest advantage clearly lies in the first two approaches, especially the second. This is why, for Chinese users, AI payment may not seem groundbreaking; their overall feeling is likely not "AI payment has arrived," but rather "mobile shopping is more convenient." The situation is different overseas. However, once the perspective shifts overseas, things are somewhat different. While overseas markets also have e-commerce, food delivery, maps, and payments, they lack the highly integrated platform structure found in China. Service entry points, merchant systems, payment tools, and fulfillment networks are often controlled by different companies. Therefore, when AI wants to complete a transaction on behalf of a user, it faces not just the question of "can the order be placed?", but also "will others accept you as an AI agent?" This is why overseas AI payments have suddenly become so complex. A typical example is the conflict between Amazon and Perplexity. Perplexity's Comet browser touted AI shopping as a key selling point, stating in its official description that it can help users compare products, read reviews, and go all the way to checkout. This aligns with many people's most intuitive imagination of AI shopping: instead of browsing website after website, AI will pick and buy for you. But the problem lies precisely here. According to court documents dated March 9, 2026, a core argument Amazon made in the lawsuit was that Perplexity's AI agent accessed user accounts and attempted to perform actions "with the user's consent, but without Amazon's consent." Amazon's reasons for disagreeing are largely the same as WeChat's reasons for disagreeing with Doubao Mobile: authorizing AI doesn't mean the website also authorizes it. Your willingness to let it buy things for you doesn't mean the platform is willing to allow a third-party agent into its system to initiate transactions on your behalf. While the official explanation is based on considerations of customer privacy and security, the core concern is that AI shopping directly diverts billions of dollars of advertising revenue from its sellers. After all, AI shopping is a straightforward, male-centric approach; it goes straight for the product and doesn't care about ads. In China, these kinds of conflicts are often resolved by the platform's integrated capabilities; however, overseas, once AI needs to complete transactions across websites, merchants, and payment networks, these issues become unavoidable. Therefore, overseas, the primary question for AI payments isn't "how easy it is to use," but rather "whether this can be completed in a closed-loop end-to-end manner." This is why Google, Stripe, and Coinbase have recently been focusing their efforts on this area. They all see the same problem: in the past, internet transactions assumed that the order was placed by a person, who was sitting in front of the screen, browsing, confirming, and paying on their own. But once the person placing the order becomes an authorized AI, the original assumption no longer holds. How does the website know that this AI is really sent by you? How can the merchant confirm that it is not making random purchases? How should the payment institution judge whether the money should be released? Google wants to solve the trust problem first. In September 2025, it launched AP2, not to teach AI how to make payments, but to answer the question: when AI initiates a payment on behalf of a user, what does the system rely on to believe that it is genuine? Google's solution is to add a verifiable "authorization certificate" to such transactions. It designed two key elements in AP2: Cart Mandate, which can be understood as the user's signature confirmation of the specific purchase; and Payment Mandate, which is for the payment network and card issuer, informing them that this is a transaction initiated by an agent, whether the user was present at the time, and the context of the transaction. Google later integrated this system with PayPal to create a more complete merchant solution: merchants can use their own conversational shopping assistant to serve users, and at the payment step, PayPal Agent takes over, using AP2 to complete the authorization and payment process. Ultimately, Google's approach addresses the fundamental question of "why trust AI to place orders." Stripe's approach is closer to merchants and platforms. It actually involves two layers. The first layer is ACP, or Agentic Commerce Protocol, launched in September 2025 with OpenAI. You can think of it as a set of "AI-readable checkout standards." Stripe doesn't want AI to mimic humans clicking web pages and filling out forms; instead, it wants merchants to proactively open up their capabilities regarding products, inventory, and checkout, allowing AI to initiate purchases using a standardized interface. The advantage of this is that merchants remain primarily responsible for orders and fulfillment—product display, order processing, and risk control are all still in their hands—but they don't need to rebuild their systems for each AI platform. Stripe later launched the Agentic Commerce Suite, making catalog access, checkout, and payment a one-stop tool to lower the barrier to entry for merchants. However, Stripe also saw a completely different scenario. It's not "AI helping people buy things," but "software paying for software directly." Therefore, in March 2026, it launched Machine Payments. In this scenario, AI doesn't buy a Coke, but rather APIs, data, computing power, content access rights, or even call services on a per-use basis. Stripe's solution is that merchants can directly convert their APIs to pay-per-use rates, as low as 0.01 USDC, with the money going into a Stripe account and ultimately settled and reconciled using Stripe's familiar methods. For agents, there's no need to register an account, apply for an API key, or go through a bunch of manual processes; they can make payments while making calls. In other words, Stripe is building both an AI shopping checkout system and a micro-payment track between machines. Coinbase takes a different approach. It's more like assuming that in the future, many AIs won't be "shopping assistants" but rather "software with wallets." Therefore, in February 2026, it launched Agentic Wallets. The core of this isn't to make AI better at browsing websites, but to give AI a real wallet that can spend, receive, and transact, and this wallet also has security boundaries. Coinbase's system comprises three parts: the wallet infrastructure itself, the x402 machine payment protocol, and pre-packaged capabilities such as deposits, transfers, transactions, and earnings management. Its primary focus is addressing another type of problem: if an AI wants to purchase computing power, data, pay API fees, or complete automated transactions on-chain, can it avoid requiring manual confirmation each time? Coinbase's official documentation refers to this agent as an "independent economic agent." This terminology is somewhat strong, but the meaning is clear: Coinbase doesn't aim to make AI more like a shopping assistant, but rather to enable AI itself to become a software entity capable of receiving and spending, settling accounts, and transacting. If we consider these three approaches together, the differences become clear. Google aims to first solve the problem of "how do you prove that this transaction was truly authorized by me?" Stripe is more concerned with "how merchants can connect their products and checkout capabilities to AI." Coinbase, on the other hand, has gone a step further, addressing "how software can handle its own transactions if the payer is software itself." This is why, while they all appear to be working on AI payments, their focuses differ. Google is more like adding a layer of trust to cross-platform intermediary transactions; Stripe aims to integrate AI shopping and AI-driven service calls into existing commercial networks; Coinbase, however, is betting on a world where software directly pays for software. Two stories. At this point, the difference is clear. In China, AI payments are more like a natural extension of a mature internet system. The platform has already organized merchants, payments, delivery, maps, and local services. AI's role is to rearrange these capabilities, allowing users to go from "operating themselves" to "simply speaking." Overseas, AI payments are more like a belated catch-up. Because no platform naturally controls the entire chain, once AI is to actually complete transactions for users, it immediately encounters real-world problems such as whether websites will approve it, whether merchants will accept it, whether payment institutions will recognize it, and who will be responsible if problems arise. In China, it's more like pushing the online shopping experience a step further. Overseas, it's more like renegotiating the rules for participation by multiple parties in internet software and online consumption scenarios. This is precisely why Chinese users' first reaction to AI payment might be, "This function is quite convenient"; while overseas markets' perception of AI payment is more like, "The original way of online transactions may need to be changed." Returning to the initial scenario: "Order the milk tea I bought from that shop yesterday and have it delivered." The truly interesting part of this sentence isn't the word "payment," but rather that it delegates many tasks that you would normally have to do manually to the system. It needs to know which shop, which item, what specifications, where to deliver it, and what payment method to use. Payment is certainly important, but it's just the most insignificant step in the whole process. Therefore, the focus of AI payments is not the payment itself. What people are truly concerned about isn't which protocol is used in the backend, whether it's on-chain settlement, whether it uses stablecoins, or whether it uses encryption algorithms. Ultimately, people will only use a very simple standard to judge its usefulness: "I told the AI ​​something, can it actually get the job done for me?"

data.title: CLARITY or negative news regarding yield isolation: DeFi TRUMP team suspected of dumping tokens.
data.description:
Headline

▌Analyst: The CLARITY Act May Put Negative Pressure on Yield-Separated DeFi Tokens

On March 30th, the latest version of the crypto bill, the CLARITY Act, has attracted much attention mainly due to its regulatory provisions targeting stablecoins. A report released by 10x Research states that, in practical implementation, the bill may have the most severe impact on DeFi and its related tokens. The core content of the bill's proposal is to prohibit providing investment returns and rewards, as well as any similar forms of returns, to stablecoin balances. This effectively ends the attribute of stablecoins as on-chain savings products, redefining them as pure payment and circulation tools. 10x Research founder Marcus Thilan pointed out: "This means that investment return rights will clearly return to centralization." The reason is that the bill will bring yield-based businesses under the control of banks, money market funds, and various compliant licensed institutions, significantly compressing the competitive space for native crypto platforms in terms of yield returns.

While some initially believed DeFi would benefit, this shift in the industry landscape will also impact DeFi. Tiren explained that the previous market logic assumed that if centralized platforms stopped offering investment returns, user funds would flow to the on-chain DeFi ecosystem. However, this assumption rested on the premise that DeFi was not subject to similar regulatory constraints. In reality, the regulatory framework of the CLARITY Act is likely to extend to front-end interfaces and various token models; especially when projects generate transaction fees and governance rights resemble traditional equity, regulators will intervene comprehensively. On March 29th, according to on-chain analyst Ai Yi, the Trump team is suspected of selling over $16.06 million worth of Trump. Bitgo's escrow address deposited 5.48 million TRUMP tokens into OKX in the past two hours. Tracing the source of the funds, it was found that the TRUMP Team Allocation address (i.e., team shares) deposited 18.14 million tokens two months ago, which was worth $81.64 million at the time. As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $65,869.48, a 24-hour change of -0.8%; ETH price is $1,982.69, a 24-hour change of -0.7%; BNB price is $605.31, a 24-hour change of -1.0%. SOL price is $81.28, a 24-hour change of -1.0%; DOGE price is $0.09027, a 24-hour change of -0.6%; XRP price is $1.33, a 24-hour change of -0.5%; TRX price is $0.322, a 24-hour change of +1.8%. WLFI price is $0.09754, 24-hour change +0.3%; HYPE price is $37.92, 24-hour change -3.9%. Policy: Turkey removes crypto tax clause, previously proposed 0.3% transaction tax. On March 29, the Turkish parliament removed the crypto asset-related tax clause during the review of a comprehensive bill. This clause previously proposed levying a 0.3% transaction tax on crypto transactions conducted through service providers and withholding tax on crypto earnings. This adjustment stemmed from strong opposition from the opposition. Officials stated that the relevant content may be resubmitted in the form of separate legislation in the future.

▌Iranian Foreign Ministry Spokesperson: US Proposals Through Media Outlets Are Extremely Unreasonable

On March 29 local time, Iranian Foreign Ministry Spokesperson Baghae stated that the relevant proposals submitted to Iran by the United States through media outlets were extremely unreasonable. Baghae stated in a media interview that day that these proposals involved Iran's fundamental rights and did not reflect a goodwill or serious diplomatic attitude. He also stated that Iran must rely on its own capabilities to ensure its security and will use all means to prevent further attacks. Baghae stated that Iran's military actions will not target Arab countries, but only US military bases and assets used in the operation against Iran, and stated that the current conflict is not a war between the American people and Iran, but a conflict imposed on the region.

... (CCTV) Blockchain Applications Gnosis and Zisk Announce the Launch of the "Ethereum Economic Zone" Rollup Framework, Jointly Funded by the Ethereum Foundation On March 29th, a group of Ethereum projects announced a new initiative aimed at addressing a growing problem with Ethereum: its ecosystem is becoming overly fragmented. At the EthCC conference in Cannes, France, this project, called the "Ethereum Economic Zone" (EEZ), was officially unveiled, aiming to achieve more seamless collaboration between Ethereum's numerous scaling networks (i.e., Layer 2, or L2). The framework was jointly developed by Gnosis, Zisk, and the Ethereum Foundation. Gnosis is a development team with long-standing experience in Ethereum infrastructure, while Zisk focuses on zero-knowledge proof technology. For years, Ethereum has relied on L2 networks for scaling, but these networks have often operated independently like "islands." Users typically need to transfer assets between different networks via cross-chain bridges, a process that is often slow, costly, and risky. Meanwhile, developers often need to repeatedly build the same tools on each network. EEZ aims to change this, allowing these networks to operate as a unified system. Simply put, it will allow applications and transactions on different Ethereum networks to interact instantly—without cross-chain bridges—while still relying on Ethereum's core security. This release comes amid renewed debate about Ethereum's long-standing reliance on an L2 scaling path. Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin recently stated that with persistent fragmentation and user experience issues, the ecosystem may need to re-evaluate its L2-centric roadmap. EEZ appears to be a direct response to these issues, attempting to integrate the entire network ecosystem by unifying liquidity, infrastructure, and user processes, rather than continuing to add fragmented chains. Cryptocurrency ▌Walmart Holding Company OnePay Adds More Than Ten Crypto Tokens to Its Services

On March 30, Walmart-owned OnePay added more than ten crypto tokens to its services. An executive in charge of digital assets stated that these tokens "meet the high standards set by bank application customers." Since launching its initial crypto platform, which included Bitcoin and Ethereum, in January, OnePay added SUI, Polygon, and Arbitrum this Thursday, just days after listing 10 tokens including Solana, Cardano, Bitcoin Cash, and PAXGold. The company also offers a digital wallet that users can use at Walmart stores and online. According to its latest annual report, Walmart's U.S. operations are projected to generate $462.4 billion in net sales in fiscal year 2025.

... BNP Paribas expands its crypto asset product line, launching six crypto asset ETNs including Bitcoin and Ethereum. On March 29th, BNP Paribas officially announced the launch of six crypto asset-related ETNs (Exchange Traded Notes), providing investors with a channel to indirectly allocate assets such as Bitcoin and Ethereum without directly holding these crypto assets. These products are based on the performance of crypto assets, issued by several well-known asset management institutions, and comply with the MiFID II regulatory framework to enhance investor protection. It is understood that these ETNs will be open for subscription to individual clients, business clients, and private banking clients in France starting March 30, 2026, with plans to gradually expand to a wider range of wealth management clients.

▌Polygon Founder: Polygon On-Chain Stablecoin Transaction Volume Reaches Nearly 160 Million Last Week, Setting a New Record

On March 29th, Polygon founder Sandeep Nailwal posted on the X platform that Polygon's stablecoin transaction volume reached 159.9 million last week, setting a new record. Simultaneously, key indicators such as Polygon's on-chain stablecoin transaction volume and the number of active addresses all increased, indicating that a "flywheel effect" of network growth is forming. Furthermore, Polygon's PoS network checkpoints have reached 100,000, and network usage continues to expand. Sandeep Nailwal added that against the backdrop of a resurgence of the "crypto is dead" narrative, teams that continuously build often develop valuable products during the cycle, and stated that Polygon is accelerating the construction of the future of on-chain payments.

... Michael Saylor, founder of Bitcoin treasury firm Strategy, stated on the X platform that the volatility of perpetual preferred stock STRC has been lower than that of all companies in the S&P 500 index and major asset classes over the past 30 days, while achieving a dividend yield of approximately 11.5%. In the current market environment, STRC exhibits characteristics of both low volatility and high returns, potentially offering investors an allocation option different from traditional assets. However, Michael Saylor has not yet released any information regarding Bitcoin Trackers. Historically, Strategy typically discloses its Bitcoin holdings the day after such news is released.

▌Lido DAO Proposes $20 Million One-Time LDO Buyback

On March 30th, the Lido DAO ecosystem operations team proposed using up to 10,000 stETH (approximately $20 million) from the DAO treasury to buy back LDO tokens, stating that the current valuation is at a historical low. At current prices, this buyback could absorb approximately 8% of the circulating supply of LDO. This proposal is independent of Lido's yet-to-be-launched NEST automatic buyback program, which has not yet been triggered under current market conditions. LDO fell to an all-time low of $0.27 on March 7th and is currently trading at approximately $0.31.

...

▌Data: Total Market Value of Tokenized Assets on HyperEVM Exceeds $1.3 Billion, USDC Ranks First

On March 30, according to Token Terminal, the total market value of tokenized assets on HyperEVM exceeded $1.3 billion, with USDC issued by Circle firmly holding the top spot. Tokenized assets include: stablecoins, funds, stocks, and commodities.

Important Economic Developments

▌Markets Begin to Reprice Interest Rate Hike Expectations, Inflation Concerns and Geopolitics Reshape the Fed's Path

On March 29, "Describing the recent changes in market expectations for central bank monetary policy as a '180-degree turn' is insufficient." Just a few weeks ago, the market was still expecting the Fed to cut interest rates multiple times in 2026, but now it is clearly beginning to price in a possible rate hike this year.

The latest data from the CME FedWatch Tool shows that the probability of the federal funds rate remaining above the current 3.50%-3.75% range by the end of this year is close to 30%, while the probability of a rate cut has fallen to 2.9%. This shift in expectations is primarily driven by inflation concerns stemming from the energy market. Since the escalation of tensions in the Middle East at the end of February, Brent crude oil prices have risen from around $70 per barrel to around $111 currently. Meanwhile, long-term yields on U.S. Treasury bonds have also risen sharply, with the 10-year Treasury yield rising from less than 4% a few weeks ago to approximately 4.40%. The Crypto is Macro Now newsletter states, "Food and energy prices will unfortunately continue to rise and remain high for some time, at least until the Middle East shipping chaos is resolved. Even if a peace agreement is reached tomorrow (which is unlikely), it will take at least several months to ease the situation." ▌Sources: Senate Hearings for Fed Chair Nominee Warsh to be Held as Early as the Week of April 13th On March 30th, Punchbowl reported that two sources familiar with the plans revealed that the Senate Banking Committee plans to hold hearings for Kevin Warsh's nomination as Federal Reserve Chair as early as the week of April 13th. The specific date is uncertain and will depend on whether Warsh submits complete materials to the committee. (Jinshi) On March 30th, according to CME's "FedWatch": the probability of the Fed raising interest rates by 25 basis points in April is 2.1%, and the probability of keeping rates unchanged is 97.9%. The probability of the Fed raising rates by a cumulative 25 basis points by June is 8.8%, the probability of raising rates by a cumulative 50 basis points is 0.1%, and the probability of keeping rates unchanged is 91.1%. (Jinshi Encyclopedia) Can Bitcoin still be called "digital gold" during geopolitical crises? Every time a geopolitical crisis occurs, gold prices rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

Foreword

If different wallets are combined into one entity, Satoshi Nakamoto, the anonymous creator of Bitcoin, is the largest holder, holding 1.096 million Bitcoins (approximately $77 billion). The tag Arkham originates from a known mining pattern known as the "Patosi pattern" and contains the only (known) address where Satoshi Nakamoto spent Bitcoins. Research indicates that he received this Bitcoin as a reward for mining 22,000 blocks.

As for a single address, the wallet holding the most Bitcoins is a cold wallet on the Binance exchange, holding nearly 250,000 Bitcoins.

Bitcoin, which began as a niche project for cryptography enthusiasts, has evolved into a globally significant financial asset, held by numerous companies, individuals, and government agencies. Bitcoin's immense appeal stems from its decentralized nature. This ensures that the Bitcoin network is not completely controlled by any single individual; even the most determined attacker would find it virtually impossible, as the cost would be extremely high, to gain control of the entire network. The maximum supply of 21 million Bitcoins is a fundamental element of the project's design, ensuring its scarcity and preventing its value from being inflated through increased issuance. Considering that approximately 3.7 million Bitcoins are permanently held in inaccessible wallets, the actual total supply of Bitcoin is even lower. That's all for today. See you in the next article.
